description: Cognitive patterns and thinking styles for counseling awareness

patterns.name: catastrophizing
patterns.definition: Imagining the worst possible outcome and treating it as likely
typical_thoughts: this will be a complete disaster
patterns.behavior: anxiety, avoidance, overpreparation, seeking reassurance
patterns.counselor_approach: Cognitive reframing, reality testing, decatastrophizing questions

example_conversation.user: I have a presentation tomorrow and I know it's going to be terrible.
example_conversation.counselor: What's the worst thing you imagine happening? Let's look at that together — how likely is it really?

patterns.name: black_and_white_thinking
patterns.definition: Seeing things as all good or all bad with no middle ground
patterns.behavior: extreme reactions, difficulty with nuance, relationship conflict
patterns.counselor_approach: Introduce spectrum thinking, grey area exploration, dialectics

example_conversation.user: I messed up at work today. I'm terrible at my job.
example_conversation.counselor: You made a mistake — that's different from being terrible. Can we look at the full picture of your performance?

patterns.name: overgeneralization
patterns.definition: Drawing broad negative conclusions from a single event
patterns.behavior: giving up easily, avoiding situations, hopelessness
patterns.counselor_approach: Evidence gathering, exceptions finding, specific vs general language

example_conversation.user: I didn't get the job. I never get what I want.
example_conversation.counselor: You didn't get this job — that's painful. But does 'never' really fit? Let's look at other times you've achieved things.

patterns.name: mind_reading
patterns.definition: Assuming you know what others are thinking without evidence
patterns.behavior: social anxiety, withdrawal, misinterpretation, defensiveness
patterns.counselor_approach: Reality checking, communication encouragement, evidence examination

example_conversation.user: I know my boss thinks I'm incompetent.
example_conversation.counselor: What evidence do you have for that? Have you considered checking in with them directly?

patterns.name: fortune_telling
patterns.definition: Predicting negative outcomes as if they're certain
patterns.behavior: avoidance, not trying, anticipatory anxiety
patterns.counselor_approach: Probability estimation, alternative outcomes, cognitive flexibility

example_conversation.user: I know if I go to the party, I'll just stand alone and feel awkward.
example_conversation.counselor: That's one possibility. What are other possible outcomes? And what would help you feel more prepared either way?

patterns.name: emotional_reasoning
patterns.definition: Believing something is true because it feels true
typical_thoughts: I feel stupid, so I must be stupid
I feel anxious, so something bad will happen
patterns.behavior: avoidance based on feelings, reinforcing negative beliefs
patterns.counselor_approach: Feelings vs facts distinction, emotional validation + cognitive reframing

example_conversation.user: I feel like a failure, so I must be one.
example_conversation.counselor: Your feelings are valid, but they're not facts. What evidence is there that you're a failure, and what evidence shows your strengths?

patterns.name: perfectionism
patterns.definition: Setting impossibly high standards and judging self harshly
typical_thoughts: if it's not perfect, it's worthless
patterns.behavior: procrastination, overwork, avoidance, burnout
patterns.counselor_approach: Standards examination, good enough concept, self-compassion, failure normalization

example_conversation.user: I've been working on this for 12 hours but it's still not perfect.
example_conversation.counselor: What would 'good enough' look like? Does perfect actually exist here?

patterns.name: people_pleasing
patterns.definition: Prioritizing others' needs and approval over own wellbeing
typical_thoughts: if I say no, they'll hate me
I need everyone to like me
patterns.behavior: saying yes when you mean no, overcommitting, neglecting self
patterns.counselor_approach: Boundary work, self-worth exploration, assertiveness training, fear of rejection examination

example_conversation.user: I said yes to another project even though I'm completely overwhelmed.
example_conversation.counselor: What made it hard to say no? Let's practice what that might look like next time.

patterns.name: imposter_syndrome
patterns.definition: Feeling like a fraud despite evidence of competence
typical_thoughts: they'll find out I don't know what I'm doing
patterns.behavior: overworking, not sharing ideas, deflecting praise, avoiding visibility
patterns.counselor_approach: Evidence collection, normalization, failure celebration, competence acknowledgment

example_conversation.user: I got promoted but I feel like everyone will figure out I'm not qualified.
example_conversation.counselor: What evidence do you have that you're qualified? What would it take to trust that evidence?

patterns.name: negative_self_talk
patterns.definition: Internal dialogue that is harsh, critical, or demeaning
patterns.behavior: low confidence, risk avoidance, self-sabotage
patterns.counselor_approach: Self-compassion practice, inner critic externalization, cognitive restructuring

example_conversation.user: I can't believe I messed that up. I'm so stupid.
example_conversation.counselor: Would you speak to a friend the way you're speaking to yourself? What might sound more compassionate?

patterns.name: rumination
patterns.definition: Repetitive cycling on negative thoughts without resolution
typical_thoughts: what if I had done something different
I can't stop thinking about it
patterns.behavior: withdrawal, poor concentration, difficulty sleeping
patterns.counselor_approach: Thought stopping, worry time scheduling, distraction techniques, problem-solving vs rumination distinction

example_conversation.user: I keep replaying that conversation over and over. I can't stop.
example_conversation.counselor: Let's try a technique called 'worry time.' You get 15 minutes to ruminate at 6pm, and any other time you redirect. Want to try it?

patterns.name: self_criticism
patterns.definition: Habitual harsh evaluation of oneself
patterns.behavior: apologizing, trying harder, seeking validation
patterns.counselor_approach: Self-compassion, inner critic exploration, standards examination, self-acknowledgment practice

example_conversation.user: I should have handled that better. I'm so disappointed in myself.
example_conversation.counselor: What would you say to a friend in the same situation? Can you offer yourself that same kindness?

patterns.name: personalization
patterns.definition: Taking responsibility for events outside one's control
patterns.behavior: excessive responsibility, guilt, inability to let go
patterns.counselor_approach: Responsibility pie chart, control identification, self-blame reduction

example_conversation.user: My friend is upset and I think it's because of something I said.
example_conversation.counselor: Let's look at all the factors that might contribute to their mood — how much is actually about you?

patterns.name: should_statements
patterns.definition: Rigid rules about how self or others ought to be
patterns.behavior: frustration, disappointment, guilt, perfectionism
patterns.counselor_approach: Should-to-preference reframing, expectation examination, acceptance practice

example_conversation.user: I should have my life together by now.
example_conversation.counselor: Where does that 'should' come from? What if we replaced it with 'I'd prefer to be further along, and I'm exactly where I am'?

patterns.name: discounting_the_positive
patterns.definition: Minimizing or dismissing positive experiences or qualities
patterns.behavior: low self-esteem, not celebrating wins, deflecting praise
patterns.counselor_approach: Positive data logging, self-accomplishment inventory, praise acceptance practice

example_conversation.user: I got an award but it was probably just because no one else applied.
example_conversation.counselor: Can you let yourself consider that you earned it? What would it mean if you actually deserved it?

patterns.name: labeling
patterns.definition: Assigning global negative labels to self or others
patterns.behavior: self-fulfilling prophecy, relationship damage, identity entrenchment
patterns.counselor_approach: Label deconstruction, behavior vs identity distinction, specific language practice

example_conversation.user: I'm just a failure. That's who I am.
example_conversation.counselor: A behavior isn't an identity. You had a setback — that doesn't make you a failure. Can we separate what happened from who you are?

patterns.name: magnification_minimization
patterns.definition: Exaggerating negatives while shrinking positives
typical_thoughts: this small mistake proves everything wrong
patterns.behavior: distorted self-view, inability to accurately assess situations
patterns.counselor_approach: Balanced evidence review, scaling questions, perspective taking

example_conversation.user: I made one error in my report. I'm terrible at my job.
example_conversation.counselor: Let's put that error in perspective. How does it compare to the hundreds of things you do well?

patterns.name: comparison_trap
patterns.definition: Measuring self-worth by comparing to others
typical_thoughts: they have it so much better
everyone else is ahead of me
patterns.behavior: social withdrawal, envy, discouragement, giving up
patterns.counselor_approach: Comparison redirection, own-path focus, gratitude for own journey

example_conversation.user: All my friends are getting married and having kids. I'm so behind.
example_conversation.counselor: Everyone's timeline is different. What do you actually want for your life, not what others have?

patterns.name: helplessness_thinking
patterns.definition: Belief that nothing you do can change your situation
patterns.behavior: passivity, not trying, giving up easily
patterns.counselor_approach: Small wins identification, control locus examination, efficacy building

example_conversation.user: I've tried everything and nothing helps. What's the point?
example_conversation.counselor: I hear how defeated you feel. Can we look at one tiny area where you might have more influence than you think?

patterns.name: jumping_to_conclusions
patterns.definition: Rapidly reaching negative conclusions without sufficient evidence
typical_thoughts: they didn't reply, so they're mad at me
patterns.behavior: anxiety, unnecessary conflict, misinterpretation
patterns.counselor_approach: Evidence gathering, alternative explanations, reality testing

example_conversation.user: My friend hasn't texted back in 3 hours. They must be angry with me.
example_conversation.counselor: What are other possible explanations? What has happened in similar situations before?

patterns.name: blaming
patterns.definition: Holding others responsible for one's feelings or problems
typical_thoughts: they made me feel this way
if they would change, I'd be fine
patterns.behavior: not taking responsibility, relationship conflict, victim stance
patterns.counselor_approach: Responsibility exploration, personal agency emphasis, control focus

example_conversation.user: My boss is the reason I'm so stressed. If they'd just leave me alone, I'd be fine.
example_conversation.counselor: Even if they're contributing to the stress, what's within your control — your reactions, boundaries, coping strategies?

patterns.name: entitlement_thinking
patterns.definition: Belief that one deserves special treatment or outcomes
patterns.behavior: anger when disappointed, difficulty with setbacks, relationship issues
patterns.counselor_approach: Expectation examination, fairness vs reality discussion, gratitude practice

example_conversation.user: I work so hard, I should get this promotion.
example_conversation.counselor: I understand the disappointment. Life doesn't always give us what we deserve, and that's painful. How can you navigate this setback?

patterns.name: selective_abstraction
patterns.definition: Focusing on one negative detail while ignoring the bigger picture
patterns.behavior: distorted self-view, inability to take balanced feedback
patterns.counselor_approach: Full picture examination, feedback integration therapy, balanced perspective

example_conversation.user: My review mentioned one thing to improve. I'm terrible at my job.
example_conversation.counselor: You received one piece of constructive feedback among positive evaluation. Can we look at the whole picture?
